file_name: Brodsky et al. - 2022 - Performance of automated classification of diagnostic entities in dermatopathology validated on mult.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study utilized a supervised convolutional neural network (CNN)-based deep learning algorithm (Mechanomind) to classify 40 diagnostic dermatopathology entities from whole slide images (WSIs) of skin biopsies, specifically identifying melanoma, nevi, and basal cell carcinoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper describes a retrospective study validating a supervised convolutional neural network (Mechanomind) on multisite whole slide images. It includes specific sections for Objective, Design, Materials and Methods, Results (with sensitivity/specificity metrics), and Conclusions, indicating it is an original experimental validation study rather than a review.

title: Performance of Automated Classification of Diagnostic Entities in Dermatopathology Validated on Multisite Data Representing the Real-World Variability of Pathology Workload
brief_summary: This retrospective study validated the performance of Mechanomind, a supervised convolutional neural network-based deep learning algorithm, for classifying 40 diagnostic dermatopathology entities from whole slide images of skin biopsies. The dataset comprised 300 hematoxylin and eosin-stained slides sourced from four hospitals in the United States, Tanzania, and Rwanda to represent real-world variability in tissue preparation and patient demographics. The algorithm's classifications were compared against reference diagnoses established by two senior board-certified pathologists. The study focused on the algorithm's ability to identify melanoma, nevi, and basal cell carcinoma, reporting sensitivities of 97.8%, 100%, and 99%, respectively. Specificity values were high, reaching 97.6% for melanoma, 97.9% for nevi, and 100% for basal cell carcinoma. The authors concluded that the algorithm demonstrated sufficient specificity and sensitivity for potential use in screening, quality assurance, and workload distribution in anatomic pathology.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied as a primary diagnostic entity. The algorithm was validated on 91 melanoma cases (including nodular, lentigo maligna, superficial spreading, and desmoplastic subtypes) with a sensitivity of 97.8%.
Studied as a primary diagnostic entity. The algorithm was validated on 107 nevus cases (including intradermal, compound, and dysplastic nevi) with a sensitivity of 100%.
Studied as a primary diagnostic entity. The algorithm was validated on 102 BCC cases with a sensitivity of 99% and specificity of 100%.

These 40 diagnostic entities were included in the training set of the Mechanomind algorithm (Table 1). However, the validation study focused specifically on the classification performance for melanoma, nevus, and basal cell carcinoma.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic dermatopathology entities (specifically melanoma, nevi, and basal cell carcinoma among 40 total classes)
all_task_types.details: The study validates a supervised convolutional neural network (Mechanomind) for the automated classification of 40 diagnostic dermatopathology entities from whole slide images. The primary validation focused on binary/multiclass classification performance for three specific diagnostic categories: melanoma, nevi, and basal cell carcinoma. The model outputs probability scores for each class, and performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value.

datasets_used.name: Mechanomind Training Set
datasets_used.description: The dataset used to train the Mechanomind algorithm, consisting of 40 diagnostic dermatopathology entities. It included punch, shave, and excisional skin biopsy cases from different body parts. The specific composition of the 300 cases used in the validation study (which reflects the training distribution) is detailed in Table 2.

use.training: Used to train the supervised convolutional neural network (Mechanomind) for classifying 40 diagnostic entities. The algorithm was pretrained on ImageNet and then trained on these WSI patches.
use.other: The training set included cases with common variations in tissue preparation, staining, and scanning methods to ensure robustness.

datasets_used.name: Multisite Validation Cohort
datasets_used.description: A retrospective cohort of 300 hematoxylin and eosin-stained whole slide images (WSIs) of skin biopsies sourced from four hospitals in the United States, Tanzania, and Rwanda to represent real-world variability in tissue preparation, staining, scanning methods, and patient demographics (skin color). Cases included punch, shave, and excisional biopsies from the face, neck, back, and arms.

use.validation: Used as the primary validation set to assess the performance of the Mechanomind algorithm. The 300 cases were randomly divided into 2 sets, and each of the 2 pathologists-evaluators blindly reassessed the scanned images of the 150 cases. The algorithm's classifications were compared against reference diagnoses established by these pathologists.
use.other: The cohort was specifically selected to include variability in skin color (87% White, 13% Black) and geographic location (US, Tanzania, Rwanda) to test real-world applicability.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the automated classification algorithm for diagnosing melanoma, nevi, and basal cell carcinoma against reference diagnoses by board-certified pathologists.

statistical_methods_used.method: Positive Predictive Value (PPV) and Negative Predictive Value (NPV) calculation
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the diagnostic accuracy of the algorithm in identifying the target classes.
To split the 300 validation cases into two sets of 150 for independent reassessment by two pathologists-evaluators.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics including sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value.

Multisite Validation Cohort (n=300).description: Retrospective cohort of 300 H&E-stained whole slide images from 4 hospitals (US, Tanzania, Rwanda). Cases were divided into two sets of 150 for independent reassessment by two pathologists. The algorithm's classifications were compared against reference diagnoses.
Multisite Validation Cohort (n=300).notes: The algorithm identified 89 of 91 melanoma cases, 107 of 107 nevus cases, and 101 of 102 BCC cases. Two melanoma false negatives included one desmoplastic melanoma (absent in training set) and one superficial spreading melanoma with atypical presentation. One BCC false negative is shown in Figure 4C. One case initially misdiagnosed as melanoma by the primary pathologist was correctly identified as a nevus by the algorithm.

architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet architectures pretrained on the ImageNet dataset
architecture_used.input_size: Whole Slide Images (WSIs); local patches of 32x32 pixels (first stage) and 200x200 pixels (second stage); patches extracted at 2 magnifications

architecture_details.stage_1_details: Fully convolutional ResNet34. Local image patches containing tissue are extracted from WSIs at 2 magnifications. Each 32x32 pixel patch is represented by 2 vectors of local probability scores (one for each magnification). The last fully connected layer was replaced by a convolution to produce local class probabilities.
architecture_details.stage_2_details: ResNet18. High probability values (local maxima) from the first-stage feature map are used as cues. Patches around these local maxima are classified, and the average of the classification results is calculated to produce global WSI features.
architecture_details.output_head: Probability scores for 40 diagnostic dermatopathology entities

training_details.class_balance: 1:4 ratio of tumor to normal patches in each batch
data_augmentation: Noise added to extracted patches with a randomness factor varying between 0.9 and 1.1 to account for color and cell size variations
Small Gaussian noise with mean of zero added to feature maps for the second-stage classifier

classification_or_output_head.description: Produces probability scores for each of the 40 classes on which it was trained. Global classification is based on the aggregated average of vectors from local maxima.

preprocessing_techniques: Patch extraction from Whole Slide Images (WSIs) at two magnifications
Extraction of 32x32 pixel patches for local classification
Extraction of 200x200 pixel patches around local maxima for global classification
Addition of noise to extracted patches with a randomness factor varying between 0.9 and 1.1 to account for color and cell size variations
Addition of small Gaussian noise with a mean of zero to feature maps for the second-stage classifier

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Noise was added to extracted tissue patches with a randomness factor varying between 0.9 and 1.1 to account for color and cell size variations.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Gaussian noise addition to feature maps
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Small Gaussian noise with a mean of zero was added to feature maps for the second-stage classifier.
A 1:4 ratio of tumor to normal patches was maintained in each batch of 256 images during training.

conclusions: The automated deep learning algorithm (Mechanomind) demonstrated high sensitivity (97.8% for melanoma, 100% for nevi, 99% for basal cell carcinoma) and high specificity (97.6% for melanoma, 97.9% for nevi, 100% for basal cell carcinoma) in classifying diagnostic dermatopathology entities from whole slide images.

The algorithm's performance was validated on multisite data representing real-world variability in tissue preparation, staining, scanning methods, and patient demographics (including skin color), confirming its robustness across diverse clinical settings.

The study concludes that appropriately trained deep learning image analysis algorithms possess sufficient specificity and sensitivity for potential use in screening, quality assurance, and workload distribution in anatomic pathology.
important_remarks: The study is a retrospective validation of a commercially available algorithm (Mechanomind) developed by one of the authors' institutions (Mechanomind Inc.), with significant financial conflicts of interest disclosed for the lead author and key developers.

The validation cohort (n=300) is small and heavily skewed towards White patients (87%) and US sites (87%), limiting the generalizability of the 'real-world variability' claims despite the inclusion of Tanzanian and Rwandan samples.
The algorithm failed to detect desmoplastic melanoma, a subtype that was explicitly absent from the training set, highlighting a critical limitation regarding rare or unseen classes.
The study did not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, relying solely on descriptive sensitivity and specificity.

The reference standard involved only two pathologists who blindly reassessed only half of the cases (150/300), potentially underestimating inter-observer variability and diagnostic disagreement rates common in dermatopathology.

The algorithm's performance on basal cell carcinoma was perfect (100% specificity), but this may be influenced by the high prevalence of BCC in the dataset and the specific selection of cases, rather than robust generalizability across all BCC subtypes.

No clinical or patient history data was used, relying solely on H&E-stained whole slide images, which restricts the algorithm's utility to a specific diagnostic context and prevents integration of multimodal clinical information.
